title-group.article-title: Interface Engineering Strategies for Magnetic and Magneto-Optical Enhancement of Two-Dimensional Fe3GeTe2

contrib-group.aff: † Wuhan National High Magnetic Field Center and Department of Physics, 12443Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, P. R. China
‡ School of Elementary Education, Wuhan City Polytechnic, Wuhan 430070, P. R. China

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Authors. Published by American Chemical Society

license.license-p: This article is licensed under CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0

abstract.p: Two-dimensional (2D) ferromagnets have great potential applications in spintronic devices. Critical temperature, exchange bias, and magnetic signal strength are key parameters for their application. To enhance the magnetism of 2D itinerant ferromagnets and amplify the emergence of new physical phenomena, different kinds of interface engineering techniques have been utilized to modulate the magnetism of 2D ferromagnets; here, Fe3GeTe2 (FGT) is selected as an example. The results show a 20 K increase in Curie temperature, an exchange bias of 0.016T, and an 11-fold enhancement of the maximum Kerr rotation angle. Specifically, in FGT/WTe2 heterostructures, interface-induced Dzyaloshinskii–Moriya interaction stabilizes skyrmions, whose density can be effectively modulated by out-of-plane current, as detected via topological reflection magnetic circular dichroism. In FGT/MnPSe3(MPSe), strong interfacial exchange coupling between the ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic layers produces a pronounced positive exchange bias. The Au-layer microcavity synergistically enhanced dielectric modulation and plasmonic near-fields, increasing the Kerr rotation to 16.5 mradan 11-fold boostdemonstrating the potential of Au nanostructures for magneto-optical amplification. These findings clarify the physical mechanisms by which interface engineering tunes 2D ferromagnetism and provide practical strategies for designing high-performance, low-power spintronic and magneto-optical devices based on van der Waals magnetic heterostructures.

institution-wrap.institution: National Natural Science Foundation of China
National Key Research and Development Program of China

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p:
In recent years, two-dimensional (2D) ferromagnets have attracted significant attention due to their promising applications in spintronic devices. Key magnetic parameters such as Curie temperature, coercivity, and magnetic signal strength critically determine their device integration potential. −
Among various strategies, interface engineering has emerged as a pivotal approach for tailoring material properties, playing a decisive role in the advancement of modern magnetism and spintronics. Through heterostructure design, interface engineering can induce diverse physical phenomenaincluding Dzyaloshinskii–Moriya interaction (DMI), −
exchange bias, ,, and local field enhancement , offering versatile mechanisms for manipulating topological magnetic domains, magnetization reversal, and magneto-optical responses.

Among the family of 2D ferromagnets, van der Waals (vdW) materials such as Cr2Ge2Te6
, CrI3
−
and Fe3GeTe2(FGT) −
stand out for their ability to retain long-range magnetic order down to the monolayer limit and their compatibility with multifunctional heterostructure construction. These features make them ideal platforms for miniaturized and multifunctional spintronic devices. Recent advances have revealed a variety of intriguing phenomena and device concepts: antisymmetric magnetoresistance in FGT/MnPS3 heterostructures, topological Hall effect and magnetic skyrmions in FGT/WTe2 6, enhanced interlayer magnetic coupling and elevated Curie temperature in Fe-intercalated FGT films, and pronounced exchange bias in CrCl3/ FGT/CrCl3 trilayers. Nevertheless, the thermal instability of magnetic ordering and insufficient magnetic anisotropy in low-dimensional systems often result in low Curie temperatures and weak magnetization, posing a major challenge for enhancing magnetism and emergent phenomena in itinerant 2D ferromagnets.

In this work, we construct and investigate three types of FGT-based vdW heterostructuresFGT/WTe2, FGT/MPSe, and Au/FGT/Auto systematically explore the role of interface engineering in modulating 2D ferromagnetism. In the FGT/WTe2 heterostructure, the interface-induced Dzyaloshinskii–Moriya interaction stabilizes magnetic skyrmions, which are detected and manipulated via out-of-plane current control and topological reflection magnetic circular dichroism (T-RMCD) measurements. In the FGT/MPSe system, magneto-optical Kerr effect (MOKE) reveals a positive exchange bias field up to 0.016 T. In the Au/FGT/Au structure, the synergy between microcavity resonance and local field enhancement boosts the Kerr rotation angle to 16.5 mradan 11-fold enhancement. These findings offer new insights and strategies for enhancing the performance of 2D magnetic materials, enabling low-power magnetic control and topologically protected memory applications.

sec.title: Results and Discussion
sec.p: Single crystals of FGT and MPSe were synthesized via a chemical vapor transport (CVT) method. Thin flakes of FGT, MPSe, and WTe2 were obtained by mechanical exfoliation from bulk crystals and subsequently transferred onto oxidized silicon substrates using a dry transfer technique to fabricate FGT/MPS and FGT/MPSe heterostructures. Detailed procedures for crystal growth, characterization, and heterostructure assembly are provided in the Experimental Section. FGT is an intrinsic itinerant ferromagnet with a relatively high Curie temperature (∼220 K) and strong out-of-plane magnetic anisotropy down to the monolayer limit. Its Curie temperature can be further tuned toward room temperature via ionic gating or patterning strategies. As illustrated in Figure a, three distinct types of vertically stacked heterostructures were individually fabricated based on FGT to explore interface engineering effects. These include FGT/WTe2, FGT/MPSe, and Au/FGT/Au devices, each prepared and measured separately.

caption.p: Schematic illustration of magnetism modulation in two-dimensional FGT via interface engineering. (a) Schematic illustration of three distinct FGT-based heterostructures, each fabricated separately. (b) The variation of the RMCD signal in the FGT/WTe2 heterojunction under currents of 0 mA and 3 mA. (c) Kerr rotation hysteresis loops of the FGT/MPSe heterostructure measured at 10 K after field-cooling under −1 T. (d) Typical MOKE signals for Au/FGT and Au/FGT/Au heterostructures were measured at 10 K at 570 nm.

sec.p: In the FGT/WTe2 van der Waals heterostructure, the interfacial Dzyaloshinskii–Moriya interaction (DMI) can induce the formation of Néel-type magnetic skyrmions, whose existence has been confirmed by topological Hall effect measurements and direct Lorentz TEM imaging. Conventional electrical detection methods are often limited by material conductivity, whereas topological reflection magnetic circular dichroism (T-RMCD) enables noncontact optical detection of skyrmions. In this work, we combined out-of-plane current modulation with T-RMCD to monitor the nucleation and annihilation of skyrmion domains in the heterostructure. Figure b shows the magnetic field dependence of the T-RMCD signal under zero and 3 mA out-of-plane current. A pronounced hysteresis peak emerges near 0.2 T, which becomes sharper under an applied current, confirming the effectiveness of current-enhanced optical detection of skyrmions via interface engineering.

We further investigated the magnetic proximity effect in the FGT/MPSe heterostructure using magneto-optical Kerr effect (MOKE) measurements. As shown in Figure c, field-cooling under an out-of-plane magnetic field of −1 T down to 10 K reveals a clear exchange bias. The hysteresis loop exhibits a leftward shift, with coercive fields of 0.643T and 0.675T on the left and right branches, respectively, yielding an exchange bias field (H EB) of 0.016 T. This pronounced exchange bias confirms strong interfacial coupling between the ferromagnetic FGT and antiferromagnetic MPSe layers, providing direct evidence for proximity-induced magnetic modulation in 2D vdW systems. To further clarify its origin, we compared field-cooled (FC) and zero-field-cooled (ZFC) conditions. No exchange bias was observed under ZFC, confirming that the effect arises from FC (Figure S1). Moreover, temperature-dependent Kerr hysteresis loops demonstrate that the positive exchange bias is prominent at low temperatures, gradually decreases with an increase in temperature, and eventually vanishes near the Néel temperature of MPSe. These results further corroborate the interfacial exchange coupling mechanism and reveal its thermal evolution.

Finally, we constructed a Au/FGT/Au “sandwich” nanostructure to enhance the ferromagnetic response of FGT via optical cavity effects. As shown in Figure d, MOKE measurements at 10 K under 570 nm illumination reveal rectangular hysteresis loops for both Au/FGT and Au/FGT/Au structures. Notably, the maximum Kerr rotation angle in the Au/FGT/Au device reaches 16.5 mrad, representing an ∼11-fold enhancement compared with the 1.5 mrad observed in the Au/FGT bilayer. This substantial increase indicates that the top Au layer significantly amplifies the magneto-optical response. In addition to the Kerr rotation amplitude, the hysteresis loops of Au/FGT/Au also exhibit a markedly improved signal-to-noise ratio, nearly 1 order of magnitude higher than that of Au/FGT. This further demonstrates that the Au layers not only enhance the signal strength but also improve the reliability and robustness of the magneto-optical measurements. The results further confirm that localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) excited by the interfacial Au nanostructures generates a strong local field enhancement, which effectively amplifies the magneto-optical signal. By precisely tailoring the thickness of the Au layers, the maximum enhancement can be achieved at specific wavelengths, offering a promising strategy for the design of high-sensitivity magneto-optical devices such as sensors and memory elements.

Although interfacial Dzyaloshinskii–Moriya interaction (DMI) has been demonstrated to stabilize skyrmions, the real-time phase transition pathways of skyrmion lattices under current-driven conditions remain experimentally unexplored. In this study, we employ out-of-plane current modulation combined with reflective magnetic circular dichroism (RMCD) measurements to characterize FGT/WTe2 heterostructures and investigate the evolution of magnetic hysteresis loops during skyrmion domain annihilation.

RMCD hysteresis loops were measured at 10 K by using a polar magneto-optical Kerr setup under varying out-of-plane currents. The magnetic field was swept to within ±0.6 T at a rate of 400 mT/min. Before each RMCD measurement, a strong out-of-plane magnetic field (500 mT) was first applied to fully saturate the magnetization of the Fe3GeTe2 flake in either the positive or negative direction, after which the field was reduced to 0 mT. All RMCD signals were therefore recorded under zero-field conditions, ensuring that the observed topological response originates solely from the applied out-of-plane current. As shown in Figure a, the RMCD hysteresis loop at zero current exhibits two sharp hump-like features at the edges. These topological magneto-optical signals have been previously attributed to the annihilation of magnetic skyrmion domains, confirming that interfacial DMI in FGT/WTe2 can spontaneously induce skyrmion lattice formation even in the absence of external current.

caption.p:
Topological magneto-optical response of current-modulated FGT/WTe2 heterostructures at 10 K. (a–c) RMCD signals measured under applied vertical currents of 0 mA, 2 mA, and 3 mA. (d) Schematic illustration of the decomposition of the T-RMCD signal in the FGT/WTe2 heterojunction under a 3 mA vertical current. (e) S
T /S
A ratios under different external currents, where SA denotes the area of the RMCDord image and ST denotes the area of the RMCDtopo image. (f) Coercive field variation as a function of vertical current at 10 K.

sec.p: To verify the current-tunable nature of skyrmion topological density, we introduced thick graphene (Gr) layers as vertical top and bottom electrodes and applied different out-of-plane currents across the heterostructure. Upon application of a 2 mA current, the RMCD hysteresis loop exhibited a clear contraction, with reduced coercivity and enhanced topological magneto-optical signal (T-RMCD), as shown in Figure b. Upon increasing the current to 3 mA (Figure c), the hysteresis loop continued to shrink, and the topological signal was further amplified. The corresponding RMCD images at 1 and 4 mA are provided in Figure S2.

To gain deeper insight into the current-induced modulation mechanism, we adopted a decomposition method reported in previous work and separated the T-RMCD curve at 3 mA into two distinct components (Figure d): a square-shaped conventional magnetic order component (RMCDord, inset), with area denoted as S
A , and a sharp peak-like topological magnetic structure component (RMCDtopo, main panel), with area denoted as S
T . The RMCDord component displays a rectangular hysteresis profile, reflecting the reversal of stripe domains and structural domain walls, while the RMCDtopo component exhibits antisymmetric peak features, representing the magneto-optical response of skyrmions.

Assuming that the anomalous peak area S
T extracted from Figure d represents the magnitude of the topological magnetic signal, while the rectangular area S
A in the inset corresponds to the conventional hysteresis signal, we compared the variation of the S
T /S
A ratio under different out-of-plane currents (Figure e). The results reveal that the proportion of the topological magneto-optical signal increases progressively with current, reaching a maximum at 3 mA. Beyond this point, a further increase in current leads to a decline in the S
T /S
A ratio, suggesting that out-of-plane current within a certain range may promote skyrmion nucleation and stabilization, thereby enhancing their magneto-optical response.

We attribute this behavior to the formation of a spin-polarized current flowing vertically through the ferromagnetic FGT layer. This current exerts a stronger spin–orbit torque (SOT) on the local magnetic moments, effectively lowering the energy barrier for skyrmion nucleation. Under the cooperative influence of strong interfacial DMI, this facilitates the generation of additional skyrmions, thereby increasing their areal density. This current-driven domain evolution is directly confirmed by zero-field Kerr microscopy imaging (Supporting Information Figure S3), where labyrinth-like domains gradually evolve into bubble-like textures upon applying out-of-plane current. While these Kerr microscopy measurements provide direct real-space evidence of current-induced magnetic texture modulation, future high-resolution studies using MFM or Lorentz TEM will be necessary to fully resolve the nanoscale spin configurations.

Additionally, we examined the coercivity evolution of the FGT/WTe2 heterostructure under varying out-of-plane currents (Figure f). As the current increases, the coercive field gradually decreases from 0.175 at 0 mA to 0.08 T at 4 mA, representing a 54% reduction. This trend further supports the notion that the introduction of out-of-plane current induces both SOT and Joule heating effects, resulting in a pronounced decrease in coercivity with an increasing current.

To further investigate and compare the temperature and magnetic field dependence of magnetic ordering in FGT flakes and FGT/MPSe heterostructures, we performed polar magneto-optical Kerr effect (MOKE) measurements with the magnetic field applied perpendicular to the sample surface. A laser with a wavelength of 633 nm and an excitation power of 10 μW was used for the measurements. Figure presents the temperature-dependent MOKE signals and the corresponding coercive field evolutions for both the FGT flakes and the FGT/MPSe heterostructures.

caption.p: Temperature-dependent MOKE signals of FGT flakes and their heterostructures. (a) Optical image of the FGT/MPSe heterostructure on a 280 nm SiO2/Si substrate. (b) Temperature-dependent hysteresis loops of an isolated FGT flake. (c) Temperature-dependent hysteresis loops of the FGT/MPSe heterostructure. (d) Temperature-dependent coercive fields in three distinct regions.

sec.p: An optical micrograph of the FGT/MPSe heterostructure is shown in Figure a, where the FGT flake has a thickness of approximately 25 nm, and the MPSe layer is about 16 nm thick. Initially, mechanically exfoliated FGT flakes were transferred onto a SiO2 substrate via an all-dry transfer method. The temperature-dependent Kerr hysteresis loops were then recorded by using a MOKE setup (Figure b). As the temperature increased, the coercive field gradually decreased, and a ferromagnetic-to-paramagnetic transition occurred at around 200 K. Subsequently, a MPSe thin layer was transferred onto the previously characterized FGT flake. Temperature-dependent Kerr loops were measured in both the uncovered region (i) and the MPSe-covered region (ii) (Figure c). Notably, after MPSe coverage, the ferromagnetic–paramagnetic transition temperature increased from ∼200 to ∼220 K.

A direct comparison of the temperature-dependent coercive fields in three distinct regions is shown in Figure d. After MPSe transfer and coupling with FGT, both the covered and uncovered regions exhibited a pronounced enhancement in the coercive field. At 10 K, the coercive field of the pristine FGT flake was 0.26 T, which increased to 0.55 T upon MPSe coverage.

These results demonstrate a pronounced ferromagnetic/antiferromagnetic (FM/AFM) exchange coupling between MPSe and FGT. In the directly covered region, the interfacial exchange field effectively enhances the coercive field and ferromagnetic stability of FGT, while also increasing its magnetic anisotropy and Curie temperature. Moreover, this effect can be transmitted to the uncovered region via interlayer magnetic interactions and long-range spin correlations, enabling nonlocal modulation of the overall magnetic ordering. This phenomenon indicates that FM/AFM coupling not only stabilizes magnetic order at the local interface but can also influence magnetization reversal beyond the physical contact area. Such behavior provides a new physical basis for the design of low-power, spin-programmable, two-dimensional magnetic devices and establishes an experimental foundation for exploring long-range magnetic order control mechanisms.

Finally, we fabricated Au/FGT/Au heterostructures by using magnetron sputtering and mechanical exfoliation. Magneto-optical Kerr effect (MOKE) measurements were carried out to investigate the ferromagnetic enhancement mechanism of this sandwich-type nanostructure under the influence of a Au-based optical microcavity. A schematic illustration of the device geometry is shown in Figure a. Figure b,c presents the wavelength-dependent MOKE and RMCD spectra measured at 10 K for samples with different top Au thicknesses. As the Au thickness varies, both magneto-optical signals exhibit pronounced changes in peak position, amplitude, and even sign across the spectral range, revealing a highly tunable balance between enhancement and suppression. In certain wavelength regions, the MOKE and RMCD signals evolve in a similar manner, indicating that they are governed by the same optical mode. In contrast, their divergence at other wavelengths suggests a shift in the relative contributions of absorptive and dispersive magneto-optical effects.

caption.p: Wavelength-dependent MOKE and RMCD signals in an Au/FGT/Au heterostructure. (a) Schematic illustration of the Au/FGT/Au “sandwich”-type nanostructure. (b,c) Wavelength-dependent MOKE and RMCD responses of Au/FGT/Au heterostructures with varying Au layer thicknesses. (d,e) Wavelength-dependence of the MOKE and RMCD signals for Au overlayer thicknesses of 0 and 12 nm, respectively. (f) Variation of the magneto-optical Kerr rotation angle with Au overlayer thickness at a wavelength of 570 nm.

sec.p: To further elucidate this behavior, Figure d,e compares the MOKE and RMCD spectra for Au thicknesses of 0 and 12 nm. When the top Au layer reaches 12 nm, the Kerr rotation is markedly enhanced at shorter wavelengths and becomes more stable at longer wavelengths, with the RMCD signal exhibiting a comparable trend. By fixing the wavelength at 570 nm and extracting the Kerr rotation as a function of Au thickness (Figure f), we observed a monotonic increase in the MOKE amplitude, which reached its maximum at 12 nm.

These results collectively demonstrate that the Au/FGT/Au heterostructure substantially enhances the magneto-optical response of FGT. The enhancement originates primarily from the formation of an optical microcavity between the two Au layers. Multiple reflections at the Au interfaces generate standing-wave patterns that concentrate optical energy within the FGT layer, thereby amplifying the Kerr and RMCD signals through constructive interference.

Beyond the observed enhancement, the Au/FGT/Au architecture itself represents a conceptually novel platform for engineering magneto-optical interactions in two-dimensional magnetic materials. Unlike conventional FGT/metal or FGT/insulator bilayers, the symmetric metal/FGT/metal configuration naturally forms a vertically confined Fabry–Pérot-type cavity, providing a new structural degree of freedom for tailoring the optical environment without altering the intrinsic magnetic properties of FGT.

The modulation of the magneto-optical response arises from two coupled physical mechanisms. First, variations in the Au thickness modify the optical interference conditions and reflection phase distribution of the multilayer stack, thereby tuning the effective dielectric environment and dispersion relation experienced by the FGT layer. This directly affects the local field intensity and phase delay of the incident light, enabling a controlled enhancement, suppression, or even reversal of the MOKE amplitude across different spectral regions. Second, nanoscale Au features at the interface can excite localized surface plasmon resonances (LSPR), producing strong near-field enhancement at the FGT surface. The intensified optical field couples to the spin system via the inverse Faraday effect, further boosting the magneto-optical response. By precise control of the top Au thickness, an optimal balance between interference-driven enhancement and plasmonic resonance can be achieved, resulting in substantial amplification of the MOKE amplitude at selected wavelengths and tunable control over its phase and sign.

This thickness-dependent tunability provides an important design parameter for developing high-performance FGT-based magneto-optical devices, offering a promising route toward wavelength-selective magnetic modulators, optical isolators, and on-chip magnetic sensing technologies.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In this work, we constructed three types of FGT-based heterostructuresFGT/WTe2, FGT/MPSe, and Au/FGT/Auto systematically elucidate how interface engineering modulates the magnetic properties of two-dimensional ferromagnets. In the FGT/WTe2 van der Waals heterostructure, interface-induced Dzyaloshinskii–Moriya interaction successfully stabilized magnetic skyrmions. By combining out-of-plane current modulation with reflective magnetic circular dichroism (RMCD) measurements, we quantified the current-driven evolution of magnetic hysteresis loops and extracted the topological contribution associated with skyrmion domain annihilation. The systematic variation of the T-RMCD signal, supported by zero-field Kerr microscopy imaging, demonstrates that the out-of-plane current effectively tunes the density and stability of skyrmion-like spin textures in the FGT/WTe2 heterostructure. For the FGT/MPSe heterostructure, magneto-optical Kerr effect (MOKE) measurements revealed pronounced exchange coupling and nonlocal magnetic manipulation at the ferromagnet/antiferromagnet interface, giving rise to a positive exchange bias as large as 0.016T. In the Au/FGT/Au “sandwich” structure, the microcavity formed by the top and bottom Au layers produced a synergistic effect between dielectric constant modulation and near-field enhancement from localized surface plasmon resonances, leading to a remarkable increase in the Kerr rotation angle of FGT to 16.5 mradapproximately an 11-fold enhancement. This result highlights the capability of Au nanostructures to amplify magneto-optical responses. Collectively, these findings not only provide deep insight into the physical mechanisms by which interface engineering governs two-dimensional magnetism but also offer valuable experimental evidence and design strategies for developing novel spintronic and magneto-optical devices based on two-dimensional magnets.

sec.title: Single-Crystal Growth and Characterization
sec.p: The chemical vapor transport method was used to grow FGT and MPSe single crystals. For FGT, the Fe, Ge, and Te powders were mixed in a 3:1:2 ratio with iodine in a glovebox, then sealed into a quartz tube and placed into a horizontal two-zone furnace with a hot zone at 1023 K and a cold zone at 923 K for 7 days and then cooled naturally. For MPSe, Mn, P, and Se powders were mixed in a 1:1:3 ratio with iodine in a glovebox, then sealed into a quartz tube, placed into a horizontal two-zone furnace with a hot zone at 680 K and a cold zone at 600 K for 7 days, and then cooled naturally. The magnetism of FGT single crystals was investigated by a superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID, see the results in Figure S4).

sec.title: Heterostructure Fabrication
sec.p: The FGT/WTe2 and FGT/MPSe heterostructures used in this work were fabricated by a dry transfer method. WTe2 and MPSe flakes were exfoliated onto polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) stamps and examined under a microscope, and then, the flakes with proper thickness were selected and stacked onto the FGT flake. The whole process was carried out in a glovebox filled with nitrogen. The Au/FGT/Au heterostructure was fabricated by magnetron sputtering and mechanical exfoliation. A ∼ 40 nm Au layer was first deposited onto a clean SiO2/Si substrate. FGT flakes of varying thicknesses were then exfoliated and transferred onto the Au layer inside a nitrogen-filled glovebox. Finally, a uniform top Au layer was deposited to complete the structure. After fabrication, the samples were sealed in a copper box before being transferred to the vacuum chamber inside the magnet to perform further tests.

sec.title: MOKE and RMCD Measurement
sec.p: The polar MOKE and RMCD measurements were performed using a lab-made setup (Figure S5). A 633 nm laser was used as the light source. An optic chopper and a photoelastic modulator were used to modulate the laser frequency at f DC and f PEM, respectively. The signal was collected by a preamplified photodetector connected to three lock-in amplifiers (LIAs). The reference frequencies of these three LIAs were set to f DC, f PEM, and 2f PEM, respectively. A superconductive magnet (Oxford Instruments) was used to provide a magnetic field up to 5 T and a low temperature down to 4.2 K.

ack.p: Thanks to Qinghua Hao, Menghao Cai, and Hongwei Dai for their assistance in performing MOKE microscopy experiment. This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFA1602702) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62475083). The MOKE and RMCD measurements were supported by the high magnetic field facilities at Wuhan National High Magnetic Field Center; the MOKE microscopy measurements were taken in Cloud Testing Center at Waynelabs Opto-Tech Solutions Co., Ltd.

notes.p:
The Supporting Information is available free of charge at https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsomega.5c11303.
Kerr hysteresis loops of the FGT/MPSe heterostructure under ZFC and FC conditions; RMCD images acquired at 1 mA and 4 mA; Kerr microscopy images of the FGT/WTe2 device showing current-induced magnetic domain evolution at 10 K; atomic structure of single-layer FGT and its SQUID magnetization; and schematic diagrams of the lab-built MOKE/RMCD optical setup and electrical measurement configuration (PDF)
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
